params.text:
Covert administration, self-administration and reporting errors

Covert administration means giving medicine in a disguised form, for example hidden in food, without the person's knowledge. It is only ever lawful when the person lacks capacity to decide about that medicine, and it must follow the Mental Capacity Act through a documented best interests decision involving the prescriber, pharmacist, care staff and, where appropriate, family. It must never be a quick fix for someone who is simply refusing.

Self-administration should be encouraged where possible. Assume a person can manage their own medicines unless a risk assessment — covering capacity, dexterity, safe storage and monitoring — shows they need support.

Reporting errors and near misses

Medication errors and near misses (mistakes caught before harm) must always be reported, promptly and honestly. Good services have an open, no-blame culture that treats errors as a chance to learn and improve, not to punish. Also report any suspected adverse reaction — such as a rash, swelling, breathing difficulty, drowsiness or being unusually unwell — to the prescriber as soon as possible.

params.question: A colleague realises they signed the MAR chart for a dose but cannot remember whether they actually gave it. What is the best response?

answers.text: Report it straight away as a possible error/near miss so it can be checked and the person kept safe.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Prompt, honest reporting in a no-blame culture keeps the person safe and helps everyone learn.

answers.text: Say nothing, since admitting it might get them into trouble.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Hiding a possible error risks the person's safety. A no-blame culture exists precisely so people report openly.

answers.text: Quietly give another dose to be safe.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Giving an extra dose could cause an overdose. The situation must be reported and checked first.

answers.text: Wait until the end of the shift and mention it only if someone asks.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Errors and near misses must be reported promptly, not delayed until prompted.
